sentences.text: He did not.
He decided for no money he'll go.
He hated the Communists.
He knew-- we didn't know any worse.
So Siberia-- Siberia at that time was the worst thing.
He decided he'll run away.
He'll leave everything and run away.
He'll just take the personal belongings and he'll run away.
Sure enough, he started to wander.
Originally, he wanted to go to Bialystok or to any other big city and hide himself, like, you know, in a small apartment, not in the major streets.
Nobody should know where he is and what.
I don't know how he got to Lida-- or it was he stopped there or whatever.
And he got to Lida and he found-- he decided that he's going to stay there.
And he came back home.
He told us to take the most important personal belongings.
We didn't ship our-- even our furniture, nothing.
We left everything.
Just the most necessary things, and we moved to Lida.
And that's when I came in Lida, in 1939.
What did I take for myself?
My clothing mostly, my books.
No-- no furniture, no-- no special things.
Mommy had such a linens-- special linens and-- and handmade linens with, you know, with the initials, with everything handmade.
Left everything.
I don't know what they took there, my father, her, what they packed.
But I wasn't interested really for me.
To leave the town.
You know, when my mother died, I didn't know what I'm doing.
I started to run.
There is neighbors.
It's not here, like you have to call when you come or you-- there-- there you're knocking the door and the window and you are there.
So when my mother died, I was running from house to house like a beggar screaming, my mother died!
My mother died!
You don't know that my mother died!
And then everything with the memories, I was 9 months, like I said, or a year old.
So I grew practically up there.
Everybody was my friend.
If a teacher smiled to me, I thought he likes me.
If a boy said, hello, a nice hello, I said, oh, he's in love with me.
You know, we grew up together.
I have a few friends in Israel there still from my town.
But--
No, my brother couldn't see the suffering that I'm going through because he knew how the-- you know, how they cared for me, what my parents did for me.
And all of a sudden I was a servant.
A servant.
But do this.
Do that.
Clean here.
Clean that.
Make this.
Cook this.
Clean up your-- your place.
Clean up my place.
He couldn't see in the way it was told to me, the way it was ordered.
So he said he cannot live in such circumstances.
And he went to Bialystok.
That's how I lost track on him, and I never saw him again.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So when we moved to Lida, it was a hole compared to our apartment.
Not that it was such apartments like the villas here, but it was a decent apartment.
Very nice furniture.
Very nice, you know, extremely expensive things.
And here we came in a hole because my father didn't want to be seen.
He didn't want to be recognized.
He didn't want somebody should, you know, accidentally know that he's there in Lida.
And--
It's an overnight trip for sure.
In kilometers, I don't remember and I don't know.
It was an overnight trip.
Yes.
There was no other way.
There was no other way.
And slowly, slowly we settled down.
I went back to the Russian school, you know, the [INAUDIBLE] it's called.
That's 10 years of schooling.
And I got my friends.
I was as a friendly person.
I got my friends.
And most of all-- this was night-- night courses.
And--
It was not established yet.
Don't forget that one government left, the other one came in and it was-- it was still a mishmash until everything settled down.
September.
We moved in in the late spring.
Summer, we were already there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, you know, you have to get acquainted.
You don't know nobody.
You're a stranger in town.
I never was in Lida in my life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it wasn't easy, everything in one room from so many rooms.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- but you have to make-- I was young.
I was full with energy.
He had money.
He had hidden money.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He didn't have to go to work.
Yes and no.

He worked for a little while in a-- in a store of-- you know, a painting store, like where they sell the paint and the-- like a hardware store for-- when they have to paint a house, they have all kinds of paints.
So they-- he worked there probably as a salesman, I don't know, or a helper.
I don't know what he was doing there.
No, she was Mrs.
[INAUDIBLE].
Are you kidding?
Give me, give me, give me.
Regina.
Regina.
And-- and her son's name was Grisha.
Her name was [INAUDIBLE].
I wasn't expected because they wouldn't accept me.
I was still, in '38, '39, I was still 14, 15-- 14 years old.
How would they take me to work?
Yeah, I started school.
It's different people.
It's a different system.
It's not so-- you know, it's not-- you are not as-- you didn't feel as warm as you felt in your class with the people that you started and finished it.
It's-- it's a different-- a completely different story-- strangers.
No, no.
This is-- everything was, for me, new and strange, and very pessimistic.
Very pessimistic.
Home, I didn't have any peace of mind.
The outside, I didn't like it.
The Russians were already there.
You know, I was tall and I was not-- I was-- that's what people are saying-- I was pretty.
And every Russian [? schmegegge ?] could start it up and this.
But, you know, it wasn't my kind.
I wasn't raised in this kind of atmosphere.
But one girl was with me in the Russian school, and I took a liking to her.
It will come to me in a minute.
At this moment, it's just-- I am blank with it.
In fact, I've-- I got to know Tovia from her in her house, because he was a boarder with her.
I didn't know Tovia at that time.
I just knew her.
She went with me to the same class.
And a lot of Russian people went to this class, you know, that came from Russia, from real Russia, that the parents took positions, they want transfer, you know.
My parents?
Yes.
I didn't.
Tell you why.
Because my father was traveling, like I said before.
He felt it already in Warsaw, he felt it in Vilna, he felt it in Bialystok from the-- I was sitting in one place, was a little town.
We were all together.
I had went to Hebrew school.
I had nothing to do with the public school.
That he-- he used to come home and say, I feel something in the air.
It's not what it used to be.
Especially many years to come from Warsaw.
He was very depressed.
He said, I'm always afraid.
Or he used to say, I'm afraid something shouldn't come.
This was already in '39. '38, '39, right?
Us kids?
No.
We didn't want to know.
It's the same thing like the Germans came to us and they were our friends in the beginning.
We didn't want to know.
We couldn't imagine that what would happen with us.

Not in-- not in Horodok, but in Lida already before-- before the Germans came into our region, already had a lot of refugee coming from the-- from Poland, from the back Poland, you know, like close to the borders of Germany and close to that borders.
A lot of refugees started to come in.
The same stories that we experienced, but we didn't believe them.
We couldn't imagine that this could happen.
That the killing, the anti-- the antisemitism, and all-- all what-- what they-- that here was burned, this boy was hurt, this one they throw a rock at, or this one, they took away the bike.
You know, it's always something.
In the schools, it was terrible.
Especially in the schools-- in the public schools where they were mixed, Jews and Poles.
But who wanted to believe?
When you are young, you don't want to know from nothing.
You just want to live and live up.
What are these stories are they telling you?
But when they came in one shirt and one pair of pants, and you had to take them in and to feed them, you saw that's not the story.
You saw that they're not making it up.
Were there refugees in your home?
We didn't have a home.
We went-- in Horodok, it didn't happen.
In Lida, I had one room for all of us.
Where should we take in a refugee?
My-- my stepmother he had brought to Lida-- as soon as we settled down a little bit, she brought from Vilna her mother and two sisters.
My-- my father took for them apartment, and he paid the rent for them, and they lived not far from us.
This is an individual thing.
Some of these responded very nicely to them, and some rejected them.
They [? wanted to just ?] think about it.
Because we knew about the capitalism and the communism, and my father was in there as a capitalist.
So they don't-- they didn't like them.
They would gladly take away everything, because before they sent them to Siberia, they took plenty away for themselves.
But if somebody else should have, he's a capitalist.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They didn't like business.
When I met Tovia, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's a coincidence.
He lived in my girlfriend's house in Lida.
That was the only girlfriend that I told you I took a liking to her.
I'm trying-- her name should come back to me.
No.
No.
Raja I got to know in-- in the woods.
And he had a room.
He escaped from [?
Subotnick ?] and he came to Lida too, in the time of-- of the Russians-- of the beginning-- the end of the Russians, beginning of the Germans already.
And he took a room by them.

The first house that was burned when the Russian left and the Germans came, the first house was burned completely, my girlfriend was the first one to be killed by a bomb because they heard the planes, you know, bombing.
Tovia had there, and he worked in a Russian-- a very responsible job.
He was, like, a manager.
I don't remember exactly the name of-- something had to do with-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with shipping [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH].
It means grains.
But he was in-- in-- the head of the company-- but Russian.
And he took a room by my girlfriend.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't know who he is, what he is.
I used to come to her, and she was always to tell me, we have a very nice boarder.
We have a very nice man.
He's working.
Sometimes he brings me candy.
Sometimes he brings me chocolate.
Sometimes-- and she was a year younger or a year older.
So-- and she said, I would like you to meet him one time.
And sure enough, I met him, and when I met him he promised me that he'll take us-- he'll promise us, not me-- that he'll take us one day to the movies, me and her.
And we were thrilled.
The minute I saw him, I went nuts, absolutely nuts.
I-- I thought that's the most handsome man in the world.
And he was much older than I was.
And when he was in the room or not, we used to take a ladder and look into it from outside in his room, if he's there, or he's sleeping, or what he's doing.
Especially me.
I was crazy.
Without knowing that he knows my stepmother's sister.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I came home and I told the story that I went to my girlfriend and I met a very nice man.
He's a stranger in town too, but he has a very good job.
He has a fluent Russian language.
Possesses. ?]
And he prom-- big deal, he promised to take us to the movies, me and my girlfriend.
Blah, blah, blah.
Who is the man?
Where does he live?
And she was there.
Her sister was there, and her name was Sonia.
And after I told her that, she-- she started asking questions.
How does he look?
Because she knew where he lives.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said to her, why are you asking me so many questions?
She said, because I know the man.
He was-- they sent him for some kind of a-- it's not a trip, but they send him for business-wise for a summer, to go.
And in the same train she was coming from somewhere.
And going home, they both met.
And now she started to work on me that I should be the-- not the matchmaker, because I think she was very-- she was-- she knew what she's doing herself, without my help.
But she wanted me to bring her each time some kind of news-- where he is, what he is, what he's doing.
The-- always the question was, were you at your girlfriends?
Did you see him?
Where did he go?
When did he come?
Until she started to send letters through me to him.
I never knew that between us something will happen, because this was night and day.
So I did a favor to her.

When they exchanged the governments, and the Germans started to come in and the Russians started to escape, and Lida burned one piece of fire, and we had to hide behind it-- somewhere in a village-- not in the city because it was very tragic-- he was already much closer with Sonia.
They never married.
[INAUDIBLE] said they married.
They never married.
She was his girlfriend, and he was her boyfriend, but they never married.
And they went to a peasant's-- to a farmer's house to stay over the few days that Lida was-- will be brought back to order.
And so did we, but we went separately.
Then he decided, Tovia, to go to see what's doing by his parents in [INAUDIBLE], and he took her along.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I knew him before she knew him.
She accidentally met with him at the train.
But she tried through me to accomplish all that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Yeah, it was very, very tragic for us.
But at that time, like I said before, we were young.
They were very nice to us.
We had in Lida very late a ghet-- not a ghetto-- but before the ghetto, we had certain streets.
We were divided to streets that, this is your part where you have to live.
But it wasn't a closed ghetto, you understand?
I could move around.
Yeah.
After a few days they quiet down and the Germans started to-- to come in to-- to take over Lida.
Very much damage.
Most of the city was gone.
The major streets was gone.
Completely covered.
We left Lida.
We walked to the farmer's houses behind Lida in the villages.
And there we stayed over.
You think they were so happy to keep us?
Because they had to feed us.
We left everything.
And we had-- because you-- you didn't know what-- where the bomb will be tomorrow.
Could be your house because the major streets and the train stations and every-- everything was demolished.
No, no, because it was much closer to the-- you know, by the end of the city.
You had to-- to-- very little, really, to walk to-- to leave the city.
It was the entrance from-- from the villages, from the main roads to the city.
Not too long.
Somewhere about a week.
And then we heard that Lida is-- I mean, the bombing stopped and the Germans took over.
The Germans took over, so we came back home.
Yeah, yeah.
We lived there.
We lived there.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And we lived there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say about three-quarters of a year.
Maybe half a year, not three-quarters of a year.
Then they sent us to special parts of the city where we-- where we stayed.
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah.
They took us to work.
Yeah, sure.
I didn't learn German, but [INAUDIBLE] it's a very similar-- some of them spoke Polish, very little, very bad.
But-- and some of them-- you know, the Jewish and German, it's a combination of-- of understanding here and there a word.
Maybe I didn't speak right, which I didn't.
I was afraid to put out a [? boo ?] from my mouth.
But I understood what they demanded, what they wanted.
This-- in my stage, with my friends around, they took us to work.
We used to wash their dishes, peel potatoes, fix their socks-- the socks.
If they tore something, or they had to-- to wash something, to-- to clean something, that was our work.

For that, they gave us a piece of bread, they gave us some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the-- in the cans, used to come, you know, for the military people, always comes in cans, all kind of foods, which at that time, was already a little problem with food.
They used to pay us not in money, you understand, but in products, in food, mostly.
At night, we used-- we had-- we weren't restricted not to go out in this and this-- after this and this hour, like in the ghettos.
So they used the come to a porch of my girlfriend's where we used to get together all, and sit with us and tell us jokes and-- and sing with us.
And we thought they were our friends.
This was later on.
This was later on, the yellow star.
Oh, definitely.
We weren't allow--
No, we weren't-- [INAUDIBLE] when it comes to business, this was immediately.
Jews had no right to do anything.
But we weren't restricted, you know, like in the ghetto, in a closed ghetto, not to go out, and--
No.
We worked.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah, we washed the floors.
We cleaned the-- their rooms.
They decided.
They decided.
Yes, the certain spot, which near my house was the spot where it was a whole unit of Germans that-- it was their station there.
And that's-- it was easy to get there because it was even at a quarter of a block.
And-- and they decided, you do this, you do this, you do this, you'll do that.
We couldn't decide it.
In the beginning, I'm telling you, I didn't feel anything bad.
They were very polite, they were very nice, and they didn't do nothing.
Until they-- they sent us out from there-- from this apartment, they assigned us to a special street.
Just-- we were assigned near the barracks of the-- the military barracks.
That-- it went from the Poles to the Russian.
Military barracks, a whole street.
And my stepbrother got there a job, and that's when they started already to give out identifications that you work here and here.
And he worked as an electrician at that time, because he knew some engineering.
And so he worked, and he was-- he had a special-- he had special documents that he's working for the army.
And I worked for the Judenrat
No, no, no.
That was-- no, it wasn't-- Wehrmacht, yes, but not the SS.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
Wehrmacht, yeah.
The whole picture changed.
Oh, not too long.
Maybe a few months later.
And that's when-- the minute they transferred us from the houses, to cer-- assigned us to certain streets and to certain houses, was maybe 10-- [INAUDIBLE] three or four families in one room.
That's what they assigned us to.
Yeah.
That's when they all-- the rules and regulations started to come in.
No, it wasn't in the summer.
It was already closer to the fall.
I don't know.
I really don't remember.
I think [INAUDIBLE] maybe we still were Rosh Hashanah in our apartment.
But I don't want to mislead myself, and I don't want to mislead you.
I don't know.
I don't remember.
Don't forget that I-- I had a lot of problems home, and I had a lot of problems outside.
So I was very blessed.
